question.text: If the INR interest rate rises from 7% to 8% while USD rate stays at 5%, what happens to the USDINR 1-year futures price (spot = 83)?

answer.text: It increases from approximately 84.58 to approximately 85.37
It decreases because higher INR rates attract FPI flows
It remains unchanged because spot hasn't moved
It becomes exactly equal to the spot rate

explanation.text: **The Logic:** New F = 83 × (1.08)/(1.05) = 83 × 1.02857 = 85.37. Old F was 84.58. The wider interest rate differential increases the forward premium. **The Trap:** Higher INR rates INCREASE the futures price (widen premium), even though they may strengthen INR in the spot market. **Cross-Exam Context:** Interest rate sensitivity of futures is tested in Series I and IV. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The spot rate might decrease (INR appreciates) due to FPI inflows, but the forward premium widens — these two effects work independently.
